case_title: The Education of Henry Adams / II: Boston (1848–1854) / distinction_definition
author: Henry Adams
excerpt_text:
Those of the generation of 1812 had mostly disappeared in 1850; death had cleared that score; the quarrels of John Adams, and those of John Quincy Adams were no longer acutely personal; the game was considered as drawn; and Charles Francis Adams might then have taken his inherited rights of political leadership in succession to Mr. Webster and Mr. Everett, his seniors.
Between him and State Street the relation was more natural than between Edward Everett and State Street; but instead of doing so, Charles Francis Adams drew himself aloof and renewed the old war which had already lasted since 1700.
He could not help it.
selection_reason: Selected because the passage turns on a distinction or definition that a strong reader should close around precisely. Anchor line: Between him and State Street the relation was more natural than between Edward Everett and State Street; but instead of doing so, Charles Francis Adams drew...
judge_focus: Does the mechanism identify the distinction cleanly and keep the reading move answerable to the passage?

review_history.notes: All gates pass cleanly: factual audit is clean, primary review recommends keep with all bands at strong, and adversarial review shows no high risk. The excerpt presents a clear distinction between Charles Francis Adams's natural relation to State Street versus Edward Everett's, with the passage sustaining and defining this comparison through the 'more natural than' framing and the consequential withdrawal into the old war. The mechanism is answerable to the passage text without requiring external knowledge. No concrete defects observed in the excerpt itself.

case_title: The Education of Henry Adams / III: Washington (1850–1854) / tension_reversal
author: Henry Adams
excerpt_text:
People made pilgrimages to Mount Vernon and made even an effort to build Washington a monument.
The effort had failed, but one still went to Mount Vernon, although it was no easy trip.
Mr. Adams took the boy there in a carriage and pair, over a road that gave him a complete Virginia education for use ten years afterwards.
selection_reason: Selected because the passage contains a pressure point or reversal that should reward a proportionate, text-grounded move. Anchor line: The effort had failed, but one still went to Mount Vernon, although it was no easy trip.
judge_focus: Does the mechanism stay with the reversal or tension instead of flattening it into generic summary?

review_history.notes: The excerpt presents a clean tension_reversal with the explicit 'but' structure (effort failed, but still went) that stays with the tension rather than flattening it into generic summary. The anchor line correctly identifies the reversal point. All audit gates pass: factual audit is clean, primary review recommends keep with strong bands across the board, and adversarial review shows no high risk. The passage is self-contained and provides sufficient text for probing the reversal mechanism. Ready for benchmark inclusion.

case_title: The Education of Henry Adams / XXIV: Indian Summer (1898–1899) / callback_bridge
author: Henry Adams
excerpt_text:
He shouldered his pack and started for home.
Adams had no mind to lose his friend without a struggle, though he had never known such sort of struggle to avail.
The chance was desperate, but he could not afford to throw it away; so, as soon as the Surrenden establishment broke up, on October 17, he prepared for return home, and on November 13, none too gladly, found himself again gazing into La Fayette Square.
He had made another false start and lost two years more of education; nor had he excuse; for, this time, neither politics nor society drew him away from his trail.
He had nothing to do with Hay’s politics at home or abroad, and never affected agreement with his views or his methods, nor did Hay care whether his friends agreed or disagreed.
prior_context_text: Neither he nor any of the Surrenden circle made pretence of gladness at the new dignity for, with all his gaiety of manner and lightness of wit, he took dark views of himself, none the lighter for their humor, and his obedience to the President’s order was the gloomiest acquiescence he had ever smiled.
selection_reason: Selected because the passage contains a backward callback to Adams' earlier 'false start' pattern in his educational journey, referencing his return home after Surrenden broke up as another instance of this recurring theme. Anchor: 'He had made another false start and lost two years more of education.'
judge_focus: Does the 'false start' reference function as a genuine callback to earlier patterns in Adams' education narrative, and does it connect honestly without mere associative repetition?

case_title: The Education of Henry Adams / XI: The Battle of the Rams (1863) / anchored_reaction_selectivity
author: Henry Adams
chapter_title: XI: The Battle of the Rams (1863)
excerpt_text:
Gladstone’s offence, “singular and palpable,” was not the speech alone, but its cause—the policy that inspired the speech.
“I weakly supposed … I really, though most strangely, believed that it was an act of friendliness.”
Whatever absurdity Gladstone supposed, Russell supposed nothing of the sort.
selection_reason: Selected because the passage contains a line that seems reaction-worthy but still demands selective, anchored reading. Anchor line: “I weakly supposed … I really, though most strangely, believed that it was an act of friendliness.”
judge_focus: Is the visible reaction anchored to the actual line and genuinely worth preserving?

review_history.notes: The excerpt is clean with a well-defined anchor line in quotes that generates a visible, preserved reaction about believing an act of friendliness. The bucket 'anchored_reaction_selectivity' fits correctly since the passage demonstrates both anchoring (clear quoted line) and selective legibility (the irony of the mistaken belief). No adversarial risks flagged, all bands are strong, and the factual audit is clean.
